comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Pythagoras -- well, to be more precise, it's a picture of what Pythagoras might have looked like, as seen through the imagination of J. Augustus Knapp,  the redoubtable Manly P. Hall's perennial illustrator. If anyone founded the Western esoteric tradition, it was Pythagoras, who traveled to Egypt and Babylonia as a young man, studied ancient spiritual traditions in both countries just before they were extinguished by the Persians, brought them back to the Greek world, and opened a school in the town of Crotona, in what's now southern Italy, which -- almost unthinkably for the time -- accepted women as well as men as students and initiates. (The only philosophical writings we have by women from ancient Greek times are all by Pythagoreans.). From his work, most of the classic traditions of Western occultism take their source. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though I'm going to have somewhat limited email access tomorrow and it may be Tuesday or even Wednesday before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.    

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you! See you next week.***

2. As another example, I asked, "If there is a formal religious path for me, what planet is it under?" I got Puer in the 9th house, with no mode of perfection at all. Part of the reason I was asking is that I have, for many years, bounced around 3-4 different traditions, despite all of the advice that one should pick one and stick with it. I try to pick one and stick with it; invariably, it doesn't stick. Al Biruni tells us that Mars rules idolators and those that dress in red. I wondered if the meaning was that I was meant either for heathenry and Tibetan Buddhism, neither of which I follow, and that the lack of perfection meant that neither they nor any other religion signified by Mars in Aries will become available during this lifetime. What do you think?

3. A "She loves me, She loves me not" type of chart. Fortuna Major in Houses 1 and 5; Puella in 7. Perfection is accomplished by Tristitia in 2 and 6. I would greatly prefer that She love me not. Am I right in interpreting it as "Yes, but acting on it would cause just as much trouble as you think, and it won't happen without unpleasant outside intervention, so don't worry about it"?

replies.title: Re: Some Geomancy Questions
replies.content:
1) Perfection doesn't matter if there's no yes-no aspect at all. So Mercury it is!

2) I'd read that as saying "it's way too soon for you to pick a single path." Puer is the Boy, young and impetuous; bouncing around several paths may be what you need to do right now.

3) Yep. She loves you, and you should run the other direction as fast as you can.

I have a suspicion as to why people have trouble with the idea of getting consent for magic on behalf of others, and it is simply that there are very few areas in life where we get to practice giving and getting consent, and rather that most of our lives is set for us by those in authority, whether it's a parent sending their child to pre-school, a teacher assigning homework, a boss directing work, a law mandating bike helmets, all those things are commanded by those who supposedly know what's best for us better than we do, and so when we find ourselves in a position where we can make choices of what's best for others, it's quite natural to think that we ought to do so, especially when we think we know better than they do.

Culturally, we have forgotten that we have a right to make bad choices for ourselves, and that others have that right as well.

BoysMom

replies.title: Re: Druid's Egg vs SOP
replies.content:
With the Heimlich maneuver, there was actually an extensive debate in state legislatures before "Good Samaritan" laws were passed giving ordinary citizens the right to perform that and other very basic lifesaving procedures on strangers. That took the issue out of the realm of personal consent; if you are a legal resident in the US, you have the right and indeed the responsibility to do that if you know how.

There are plenty of other situations where you don't have such rights. For example, you don't have the right to kidnap a child even if you believe the child must be taken away from its parent or guardian for its own protection. Using magic on other people falls in this latter category. You don't have the right to work magic on or for anybody else unless that person has explicitly given you consent to do so. Consent is exactly as important with magic as it is with sex!

By all means meditate on it; it's worth your while.

replies.title: Re: Druid's Egg vs SOP

This especially goes for when you're praying for the state of someone's soul - it's for eternity and thus too important to be left to mere fallible human will - and because this life is all there is before eternity in heaven or hell - since this life is your only chance at salvation - it's easily extended to praying to spare someone's life (especially the life of the as-yet-unsaved), and thus to taking actions to spare lives, even against the will of those whose lives stand to be spared. Secularized, it still works. You're not saving souls from hell anymore, but since "this life is all there is" still, the logic is still the same.

replies.title: Internet
replies.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

I am most interested in your and your readers comments on Internet addiction because, although I feel I have been moving towards greater clarity, both in understanding and in managing my Internet use, it still seems very energetically strange to me.

I find the physical energy "pull," as I would describe it, somehow different from that of TV. TV I feel in the solar plexus, and a kind of a heavy blanket of induced trance. (I gave up TV years ago and would not let one back in my house but at gun point.) Internet is another ballgame, however. Because I need the Internet for my work, financial, and social life, I am unwilling to simply eliminate it, but I have been struggling with managing my use of it ever since I started using it-- all the way back in the mid and late 1990s.

The challenge for me has been avoiding ludic loops (LLs). Everyone is going to have their own profile with LLs; for me, it is news, real estate, shopping (I don't necessarily buy things, I just love to look), and email. Oh, and Googling up whatever strikes my curiosity. None of these Internet activities are necessarily bad in themselves, but I have found myself spending more time on them than I would ever choose to.

(If any of your readers is unfamiliar with LLs I can recommend this book by the cultural anthropologist Natasha Schüll, who coined the term: https://www.natashadowschull.org/addiction-by-design/ )

About your experiment with sharp iron: Interestingly to me the sharp metal on the router and laptop seems to help, but in very specific and, to me, very surprising ways. The main result for me has been that there are three blogs, one occult and two fairly mainstream (on design and marketing) and all three of these blogs, in my considered judgement, fairly innocent-looking, that, all of a sudden, I can no longer bear to visit!! I mean, I feel a massive physical aversion towards them which I cannot intellectually explain.

I cannot say that with sharp metal objects on the router and laptop I noticed any lasting effect on my LLs, however.

In my various experiments over this year, the one thing I have found has helped me in managing my Internet use, staying away from LLs, is the Freedom app. This is basically an app that blocks the Internet, and it's been around for years-- I recall trying a crude version of it from maybe 2010 or so-- but with the more recent updates I find I can customize my schedule and specific blocks in very satisfactory ways for me.

But getting the Freedom app was only the first part of the equation. I find that what I need to do is make it a habit-- a tiny but rock-solid habit-- to first and always, always, always-- whenever I open my computer--FIRST & ALWAYS first go to the Freedom app dashboard and make sure I have the scheduled blocks in the place to protect my working time, my rest, and avoid falling into "ludic loops."

I now think of my machines that connect me to the Internet (laptop, ipad, iphone) as needing safety mechanisms against LLs-- like a car needs brakes, a front door needs a bolt... For me that safety mechanism is the Freedom app coupled with the tiny habit of ever and always first doing to the Freedom app dashboard. Not an esoteric solution, but one that has been proving more effective for me than anything else I have tried to date.

All the same, there is something to the sharp metal objects... I do think something is going on the etheric and astral levels. And I wonder if some of Jeremy Naydler's writings on electricity can begin to illuminate some of this... It still remains mysterious to me.

I am ever and always interested in your comments. Thank you for hosting Magic Monday.

replies.title: Re: Elections!
replies.content: Elections are very difficult to figure out by divination because they depend on the free will of individuals. (One of the reasons I believe in the existence of free will is that divinations, which can be very clear about so many things, so often waffle when it comes to issues affected by individual choice. The party you favor is in the first house; the opposing party is in the seventh; the tenth house is the quesited, being the house of government. See which side has a mode of perfection connecting it to the figure in the 10th house. If one side perfects by translation, a coalition with a minor party will be involved; if neither side perfects, you've got a hung parliament and either a minority government or new elections, depending on your specific system.

I was introduced to a magic course, one that seems to be very demanding and stresses the necessity of its students to be of service to planet Earth above all personal projects.

6th – Fortuna Major

8th – Via

10th – Amissio
11th – Acquisitio
12th –Tristitia

Right Witness – Albus
Left Witness – Rubeus
Judge – Conjunctio
Reconciler - Acquisitio

Laetitia in the 9th is a favorable figure and even though it does not pass to another house I took that as a “yes, I should study the course”.
Acquisitio in the 4th (end of matter) and as the Reconciler seems to confirm the answer.
The querent’s significator Fortuna Major springs to the 6th house which rules, among other things, health and services to others.
And there is Rubeus as left witness...

1 – Am I getting a warning about adverse health effects and to focus on being of service over any personal goal?
2 - L.W. Rubeus ... will the course give me a rough time? Or will it be passionate, probably with sex involved? They do not advocate drugs or other intoxicants this I am sure.
3 – Some of the Cardines are not favorable but I see Amissio as a loss of my current life as I get involved with the Course. Cauda Draconis means the conclusion of the Magic Course.

Is this a reasonable reading?

Thank you

replies.title: Re: Geomancy casting
replies.content:
OP here,

Forgot to mention that the translation of Via in the third and eight is another positive indicator.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy casting
replies.content:
The essential rule with any house chart is that if there's a mode of perfection connecting the house of the querent with the house of the quesited, the answer is yes; otherwise, the answer is no. (Yes, it really is that simple.) The house of the querent in this reading is the 1st, that of the quesited is the 9th. Is there a mode of perfection connecting those two houses? No. That's your answer.

One of the most common ways for people to mess up the interpretation of geomantic readings is to bog down in a flurry of subsidiary indications and lose track of the simple rules that give you the answer. Laetitia in the 9th simply means that the prospect seems very pleasing to you.

comments.title: Learning languages and occult knowledge
comments.content:
Language learning was something I had a great deal of interest in when I was young.

I speak English and Mandarin and studied Japanese through classes and then continued with self-study; later on, I self-studied Latin up to the level of reading Livy.

Along the way, I read a few French grammars and can parse the language at least as far as non-literary texts (academic texts etc) go. I also learned some basics of Ancient Greek, and have a rough overview of the structures of a few other languages (Tibetan, Classical Chinese) but nowhere good enough for reading anything.

As I grew up and started working, I feel like my time is ever more precious than before and I feel this dilemma between learning things that I can apply immediately in my life, which is easier done for me in English, versus learning things that will take time to unfold over a longer period of time. Language learning seems to fit more in the latter. Realistically, it would take me a long, long time of learning the required languages and literatures to say, catch up with Benjamin Dykes in terms of knowledge of astrological texts.

Would you advise someone interested in e.g. the practice of astrological magic to devote significant time to mastering Latin, Greek, and Arabic or to work with existing translations, coupled with maybe some cross-checking with the original texts where available?

replies.title: Re: Learning languages and occult knowledge
replies.content: It depends very much on what you personally want to do! One size emphatically does not fit all in this kind of work. I find a good working knowledge of Latin and French invaluable in my magical studies, but your mileage may vary.

comments.title: rubies and other stones
comments.content:
Hello JMG and all.

A year or so back one of the readers, Cliff, asked about synthetic gemstones for magical use. If this has not been discussed in the interim could I offer some advice?

I have been involved in lapidary and the gem and mineral scene for over ten years. While precious stones are clear and flawless and unamenable to most people's discretionary spending power, the majority of gems in any category are less spectacular and do not fall into the hyper-expensive range. And they are all ultimately the same mineral.

You can find all kinds of gemstones in rock and crystal shops under the guise of mineral specimens. My first taste of this was real ruby crystals of about 1cm diameter for $5 each. Opaque but genuine. Online (eg eb*y), searches for "ruby mineral specimen", "ruby crystals" or "ruby rough" will bring up large quantities of the thing in question--even diamonds--for stupidly low prices. Parcels of a few stones can be had for only ten dollars. There is no question of affordability if you do not require a gem suitable for fine jewellery.

Best wishes,

kallianeira

comments.title: Energy generation as an expression of lust, greed, pride and disdain

After thinking a while about it, I find that the way natural resources like (especially) wind and sun energy are exploited could very much be ascribed to a kind of evil that's based on pride and disdain. Pride and disdain.

Among the very many analogies to our current times one can find in "Lord of the Rings", I believe Sauron and Saruman fit very nicely in the concept of these two kinds of evil, Saruman of course being the one compelled to pride and disdain, since he thought he can just outsmart everybody, partly by technological advantage (orc that can fight in the daylight, for example). No wonder that the emblem of his power is a tall tower (I just imagined a giant rotor attached to Orthanc... )

Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Energy generation as an expression of lust, greed, pride and disdain
replies.content:
Always, but always, start with yourself. Look at your own behavior and see the places where you approach the world with a Saruman-ish attitude. (We all do it sometimes.) Clean up your own act first, and you'll find that it becomes easier to help other people do the same.

The distinction you've drawn between the form of evil that's all about lust and greed, and the form that's all about pride and disdain, is something Rudolf Steiner talked about. He labeled the lust-and-greed form Ahrimanic evil, and the pride-and-disdain form Luciferic evil. Good is the midpoint between them.

comments.title: Dee and Kelly’s “Angelic Conversations”
comments.content:
Please bear with my verbose question, JMG, as it needs a bit of a set-up. For many years I have been puzzled by the whole matter of John Dee and Edward Kelly’s “angelic conversations”, but only recently did I pick up a book devoted to the subject: Jason Luov’s “John Dee and the Empire of Angels” (which I am about half-way through). I do not doubt that angels do indeed converse with humans – albeit rarely (the exception being guardian angels). My first point is why angels (if in fact they were angels) would choose Dee and Kelly as their instruments: Dee’s basic motivation in life reminds me so much of Marlowe’s Dr. Faustus that I wonder if Marlowe used Dee as a “model” for the character of Dr. Faustus. And I don’t want to sound like a snob, but Kelly seems to me to be a “low-life” possibly with major psychological issues. My second point is that much of what the “angels” told Dee and Kelly were outright false and misleading (stuff about creating a new world religion, history as per Trithemius was soon coming to an end, etc.) and, indeed the motives of these “angels” are, to my mind, suspect. They also throw in a lot of confusion: while an angel claiming to be Uriel provided Dee and Kelly with the first lamen, later angels gave them an “upgraded” lamen stating that the first one was dictated by lying spirits! And finally, the “angels” are oftentimes harsh and severe with Dee and Kelly in ways that are appropriate from a traditional Christian perspective (something that would not likely be transmitted if Kelly was only ‘faking it’!). Even the angelic “language” appears to be controversial. My long-held view that the whole thing was an elaborate Trickster phenomenon has yet to be strongly challenged!

I gather from earlier MMs that you consider the Enochian magic that Dee and Kelly developed to be legit, but I am wondering what sense you have been able to make out of this bizarre chapter in Renaissance “mediumship” and whether the beings that Dee and Kelly encountered were, indeed, angels.

Many thanks, as always, for so generously sharing your extensive knowledge and wisdom on all things occult!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Dee and Kelly’s “Angelic Conversations”
replies.content: "Legit" is a very broad label for a very complex reaction on my part. I don't use Enochian magic at all; I did the necessary work with the system to complete my Golden Dawn studies, and stopped there. I don't think the beings Dee and Kelly worked with were angels, not least because they have a completely different "vibe" (if I may use that label) from the angels I encountered doing Cabalistic angel magic. I'll go so far as to say that I don't recommend practicing the Enochian system, and if someone wants to do so, I'd strongly advise them to learn the rest of the Golden Dawn system first, so they have the tools and skills needed to handle it.

replies.title: Re: Dee and Kelly’s “Angelic Conversations”
replies.content:
Thanks for the reply and the confirmation, JMG. I guess by "legit" I meant "actually works". For all his brilliance, Dee certainly looks like an "occult bone-head" to me: I just read that he did not use a particularly strict system for determining whether the "visitor" was an angel or demon and his buddy, Kelly, dabbled in grimoire magic on the side. Holy [insert choice expletive]! Not as though I was seriously interested in Enochian magic, but now I am backing away with my eye on the door. Whoa!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: As a high school math teacher, it warms my heart to see Pythagoras featured here. The few stories we have of his life (apocryphal or not) are wonderful for spicing up otherwise dry math lessons—the Pythagorean Theorem, for example, is far more interesting to teenagers once you’ve let it slip that Pythagoras is said to have killed one of his students for proving that the square root of two is irrational...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There are several stories about Hippasus, the student in question. One has it that he was condemned to death by Pythagoras for proving that; another was that he'd decided to go public with that proof, and while he was sailing from Italy to Greece to make the announcement, he was shoved overboard...

The issue there is a very serious one. The early Pythagoreans were rationalists who believed that the whole world could be reduced to exact number, and that if you were clever enough there were no irrational numbers at all -- everything could be worked out exactly the way that the Pythagorean theorem resolves the seemingly irrational relation between sides of many right triangles. Once it turned out that irrationality and incommensurability were hardwired into the heart of number, the old Pythagorean order fell apart, and the Neopythagoreans who emerged a few centuries later learned from their predecessors' failure and found ways to weave the rational and the irrational together in their work -- that was when the Golden Section was discovered and the square roots of 2, 3, and 5 became the three principal ratios of sacred geometry. There are lots of colorful stories woven in there!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Absolutely, and there’s a version of the story that has Hippasus being killed for going public with the construction of a regular dodecahedron as well... I’m continually fascinated by the connection between mathematics and the esoteric. Do you know of Georg Cantor, JMG? Having proven the existence of different cardinalities (sizes, if you will) of infinity, he designated the smallest one as “aleph-zero”, said to be a nod in the direction of the Kabbalah. There’re good reasons why the math department is your best chance of finding a Platonist on a college campus these days...

comments.title: Air and correspondances
comments.content:
ArchDruid,
I have been giving thought to correspondences. Much of the impetus for this has been my study of Tarot with a school that emphasizes practicing and developing personal meanings with the cards, and trying to come to terms with making correspondences between the four elements and the four houses of the Tarot (I use the Rider Waite Smith deck).

I ended up having some problems with correspondences, and I feel that although the concept is very fruitful, and I use it, it is not quite as easy and simple as it seems at first glance.

Take for example the element of Fire.
You could experience this as a sudden will to fight back: I think of Sean Connery testing Andy Garcia's character in the movie the Untouchables: Garcia responds immediately with a natural will and aggression in response to Connery's bullying.
But you could also experience something like breathing in the air outside on a beautiful new spring day, and suddenly feeling a quickening on your blood, a surge of energy. Even this simple example has more than one way of looking at it. The sudden energy and quickening of the blood is very much of the body and not so much of the mind, so many would say its associated with Fire. On the other hand, its springtime, the season of new life, and perhaps it is better associated with Air.

These two Examples seem so different to me, yet many would put them both under the element of Fire.

In the Tarot, I personally associate the Fire of the first example with the house of swords (I use the Rider-Waite Smith deck).
On the other hand, I associate the energy of the second example, the quickening of the blood, with the house of wands.

Most of the Tarot authors I have read just simply say they are both Fire, and Fire is Wands for most of them, and they don't seem to give it more explanation. I find it unsatisfactory. It seems like they have taken source material from different traditions and just jammed it up against each other, like forcing the pieces of a jigsaw puzzle to match by brute force.

I have described the issue in context of the Tarot, but it comes up in my SOP work too, and other places.

I am struggling to even ask an appropriate question here, I am not even sure what I want to know. Kind of funny!

But I will give it a try:
Would you mind briefly describing how you have come to your understanding of the elements, and if you have had any similar complications in working with them? And any other suggestions that you have not have written about in your posts on the SoP?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Air and correspondances
replies.content: I came to my understanding of the elements by way of years of daily meditation and study, and that's the route I recommend for anyone who wants to make sense of traditional symbolism. Keep in mind that the elements, like all other symbolic categories, are very rough containers into which things don't necessarily fit smoothly!

comments.title: Counting Time for Meditation

replies.title: Re: Ethical grey areas in magic
replies.content: It's bad ethics to work magic on anyone without their consent, and that includes pretty much any magic meant to affect someone else. I tend to make room for certain kinds of "cut and clear" spells, because that simply cuts the connection between the person casting the spell and someone else, and you have the right to choose what relationships you will and won't maintain. But if you're trying to control someone else's thoughts or actions -- bad idea, unless you have their consent, or in the very specific and limited case where the thoughts and actions you're trying to control involve the other person wanting to harm you. (I favor binding spells for that purpose; "truss 'em up like a turkey and walk away" is good advice.)

1) Punctuality is overrated. There was some discussion of the novena a while back, but I don't think it got completed.

2) It's discussed in Fortune's book The Training and Work of an Initiate. Basically, it's what's called karma yoga in India; you take the tasks that you've accepted in everyday life, whether that's as a homemaker (as it usually was for women in Fortune's time) or as a breadwinner, and treat them as inner disciplines; bring your best to each task, transform chores into meditations, and use your spare time wisely for study and such practices as you can fit in; accept your situation as something your karma has brought you, and work off whatever karmic connections you've got.

In her day, it was quite common for younger women to marry older men, and so there was the additional implication that you had a high chance of becoming a widow, after which your time was your own, any children you had would be out in the world, a pension would take care of your needs, and you could fling yourself into ten or twenty or thirty years of intensive spiritual practice, using all the self-discipline and meditative capacity you'd built up in the Path of the Hearth-Fire to good effect.

I've found myself using excuses, such as the fact that the divination I did before starting said I would end up doing something else. Back in the summer, I had a burst of discipline and consistency that lasted me for two straight months of not missing a single daily practice. These days I have fallen back to just doing rituals, and leaving meditation/pathwork/prayer for when I "feel like it".

Did you ever find yourself in a similar bout of uninspiration? I was considering getting in contact with Mars by a hexagram ritual and reading his orphic hymn, tomorrow at sunrise, and ask for the courage to go through with this initiatory path. The shield chart I cast asking for the result of this, resulted in puella on the right, puer on the left and conjuctio as judge. Puer sounds like it will work, but I don't get what the relationship between the three represents.

Thanks as always!

JP

replies.title: Re: Pathworking cold feet.
replies.content: Yes, this was something I had to slog through sometimes, and it became necessary more than once to really buckle down and work on self-discipline. As for your reading, it's a yes; Puella can mean passivity, so what it's saying is that yeah, you've become unbalanced in a passive direction, and invoking Mars should get you moving again.

Thank you for your discussion some weeks ago about affirmations. I'm working with one, and my wife decided to try one. Good results so far for both of us.

Mine has caused a change in direction by basically setting limits on my magic practice. Not at all expected. So, I did extra meditations and some studying. The authors strongly suggested that self examination and thinking about ancient texts (the example was the Tao de Jing) would help cultivate virtue, which they said also acts as a protection. The result of this and the other meditations was a (rare for me) aha moment. I also noticed the similarity to the discursive meditation I learned from "The Druidry Handbook".

It looks as if you mainly use the SOP these days and have healed enough from your prior debacle that you have reintroduced some Taiji? I'm glad the "energy issue" is clearing up.

One section of the Qigong reference book was about hard qigong to develop strength and abilities to absorb blows. One exercise was to take a baseball bat and repeatedly hit oneself in the head with it. An acquaintance met the main author and asked him about that. His response was that he had to do something to weed out the idiots and that nobody should do these exercises without thinking first.

Hah! That's one of the funniest stories about spiritual instruction I've ever heard; thank you. As a flake filter, that's one of the best; the only two I can think of that rival it are the Western alchemical recipe that has you make about half a pound of lethally unstable gold fulminate and then tells you to pound it up in a mortar, and the fine Chinese recipe for the elixir of immortality that uses large quantities of mercury and arsenic and tells you that when your limbs start to tremble uncontrollably, you're most of the way to immortality...

Yes, at this point the SoP is my primary ritual practice, though of course I have plenty of other options when needed, and I've begun reintegrating taijiquan into the mix. One of the things I want to do with the sacred geometry-based system discussed in the previous post on this journal is to devise something that's fully compatible with hatha yoga, taijiquan, and a few other common Asian martial arts and health exercises. We've got a golden opportunity to integrate those into Western occultism, and I want to get that process under way if I can.
